[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube district, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]].

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the [[:en:Ninju|Ninju era]] 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In [[:en:Gangyō|Gangyō]] 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine received devout support from the [[:en:Rokkaku clan|Rokkaku clan]], the governors of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the [[:en:Genki (era)|Tenshō]] era (1570–1573), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] deposited 60 oaths from [[:en:Yasu District, Shiga|Yasu District, Ōmi]] and [[:en:Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District, Ōmi]] at the shrine. In [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 3 (1594), [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]] donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the area thrived as the site of the [[:en:Moriyama-juku|Moriyama-juku]] post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju no Mori]] (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ([[:en:Amenohoakari|Amenohoakari]], {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Futsunushi|Futsunushi]])
* '''Additional Deities''': [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) - Rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.
* '''Haiden (Worship Hall)'''
* '''Ubusuna Shrine'''
* '''Kenjo Hall'''
* '''Harai Place'''
* '''Bell Tower''' - A remnant of the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period.
* '''Shrine Office'''

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Property ===
* '''Honden (Main Hall)'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.

== Address ==
〒524-0041 [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube 1-chome 8-8, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]

== Access ==
* 10-minute walk from [[:en:Moriyama Station|Moriyama Station (Shiga)]] on the [[:en:Biwako Line|Biwako Line]], operated by [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]].

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube district, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]].

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the [[:en:Ninju|Ninju era]] 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In [[:en:Gangyō|Gangyō]] 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine received devout support from the [[:en:Rokkaku clan|Rokkaku clan]], the governors of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the [[:en:Genki (era)|Tenshō]] era (1570–1573), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] deposited 60 oaths from [[:en:Yasu District, Shiga|Yasu District, Ōmi]] and [[:en:Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District, Ōmi]] at the shrine. In [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 3 (1594), [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]] donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the area thrived as the site of the [[:en:Moriyama-juku|Moriyama-juku]] post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju no Mori]] (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ([[:en:Amenohoakari|Amenohoakari]], {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Futsunushi|Futsunushi]])
* '''Additional Deities''': [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) - Rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.
* '''Haiden (Worship Hall)'''
* '''Ubusuna Shrine'''
* '''Kenjo Hall'''
* '''Harai Place'''
* '''Bell Tower''' - A remnant of the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period.
* '''Shrine Office'''

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Property ===
* '''Honden (Main Hall)'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.

== Address ==
〒524-0041 [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube 1-chome 8-8, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]

== Access ==
* 10-minute walk from [[:en:Moriyama Station|Moriyama Station (Shiga)]] on the [[:en:Biwako Line|Biwako Line]], operated by [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]].

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube district, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]].

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the [[:en:Ninju|Ninju era]] 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In [[:en:Gangyō|Gangyō]] 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine received devout support from the [[:en:Rokkaku clan|Rokkaku clan]], the governors of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the [[:en:Genki (era)|Tenshō]] era (1570–1573), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] deposited 60 oaths from [[:en:Yasu District, Shiga|Yasu District, Ōmi]] and [[:en:Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District, Ōmi]] at the shrine. In [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 3 (1594), [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]] donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the area thrived as the site of the [[:en:Moriyama-juku|Moriyama-juku]] post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju no Mori]] (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ([[:en:Amenohoakari|Amenohoakari]], {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Futsunushi|Futsunushi]])
* '''Additional Deities''': [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) - Rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.
* '''Haiden (Worship Hall)'''
* '''Ubusuna Shrine'''
* '''Kenjo Hall'''
* '''Harai Place'''
* '''Bell Tower''' - A remnant of the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period.
* '''Shrine Office'''

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Property ===
* '''Honden (Main Hall)'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.

== Address ==
〒524-0041 [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube 1-chome 8-8, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]

== Access ==
* 10-minute walk from [[:en:Moriyama Station|Moriyama Station (Shiga)]] on the [[:en:Biwako Line|Biwako Line]], operated by [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]].

When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the [[:en:Genki (era)|Tenshō]] era (1570–1573), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] deposited 60 oaths from [[:en:Yasu District, Shiga|Yasu District, Ōmi]] and [[:en:Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District, Ōmi]] at the shrine. In [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 3 (1594), [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]] donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the area thrived as the site of the [[:en:Moriyama-juku|Moriyama-juku]] post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju no Mori]] (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ([[:en:Amenohoakari|Amenohoakari]], {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Futsunushi|Futsunushi]])
* '''Additional Deities''': [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==

Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube district, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]].

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the [[:en:Ninju|Ninju era]] 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In [[:en:Gangyō|Gangyō]] 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine received devout support from the [[:en:Rokkaku clan|Rokkaku clan]], the governors of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the [[:en:Genki (era)|Tenshō]] era (1570–1573), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] deposited 60 oaths from [[:en:Yasu District, Shiga|Yasu District, Ōmi]] and [[:en:Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District, Ōmi]] at the shrine. In [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 3 (1594), [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]] donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the area thrived as the site of the [[:en:Moriyama-juku|Moriyama-juku]] post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju no Mori]] (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ([[:en:Amenohoakari|Amenohoakari]], {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Futsunushi|Futsunushi]])
* '''Additional Deities''': [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) - Rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube district, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]].

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the [[:en:Ninju|Ninju era]] 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In [[:en:Gangyō|Gangyō]] 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine received devout support from the [[:en:Rokkaku clan|Rokkaku clan]], the governors of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the [[:en:Genki (era)|Tenshō]] era (1570–1573), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] deposited 60 oaths from [[:en:Yasu District, Shiga|Yasu District, Ōmi]] and [[:en:Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District, Ōmi]] at the shrine. In [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 3 (1594), [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]] donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the area thrived as the site of the [[:en:Moriyama-juku|Moriyama-juku]] post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju no Mori]] (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ([[:en:Amenohoakari|Amenohoakari]], {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Futsunushi|Futsunushi]])
* '''Additional Deities''': [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) - Rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.
* '''Haiden (Worship Hall)'''
* '''Ubusuna Shrine'''
* '''Kenjo Hall'''
* '''Harai Place'''
* '''Bell Tower''' - A remnant of the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period.
* '''Shrine Office'''

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Property ===
* '''Honden (Main Hall)'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.

== Address ==
〒524-0041 [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube 1-chome 8-8, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]

== Access ==
* 10-minute walk from [[:en:Moriyama Station|Moriyama Station (Shiga)]] on the [[:en:Biwako Line|Biwako Line]], operated by [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]].

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube district, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]].

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the [[:en:Ninju|Ninju era]] 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In [[:en:Gangyō|Gangyō]] 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine received devout support from the [[:en:Rokkaku clan|Rokkaku clan]], the governors of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the [[:en:Genki (era)|Tenshō]] era (1570–1573), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] deposited 60 oaths from [[:en:Yasu District, Shiga|Yasu District, Ōmi]] and [[:en:Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District, Ōmi]] at the shrine. In [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 3 (1594), [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]] donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the area thrived as the site of the [[:en:Moriyama-juku|Moriyama-juku]] post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju no Mori]] (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ([[:en:Amenohoakari|Amenohoakari]], {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Futsunushi|Futsunushi]])
* '''Additional Deities''': [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) - Rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.
* '''Haiden (Worship Hall)'''
* '''Ubusuna Shrine'''
* '''Kenjo Hall'''
* '''Harai Place'''
* '''Bell Tower''' - A remnant of the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period.
* '''Shrine Office'''

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Property ===
* '''Honden (Main Hall)'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.

== Address ==
〒524-0041 [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube 1-chome 8-8, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]

== Access ==
* 10-minute walk from [[:en:Moriyama Station|Moriyama Station (Shiga)]] on the [[:en:Biwako Line|Biwako Line]], operated by [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]].

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube district, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]].

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the [[:en:Ninju|Ninju era]] 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In [[:en:Gangyō|Gangyō]] 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine received devout support from the [[:en:Rokkaku clan|Rokkaku clan]], the governors of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the [[:en:Genki (era)|Tenshō]] era (1570–1573), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] deposited 60 oaths from [[:en:Yasu District, Shiga|Yasu District, Ōmi]] and [[:en:Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District, Ōmi]] at the shrine. In [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 3 (1594), [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]] donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the area thrived as the site of the [[:en:Moriyama-juku|Moriyama-juku]] post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju no Mori]] (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ([[:en:Amenohoakari|Amenohoakari]], {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Futsunushi|Futsunushi]])
* '''Additional Deities''': [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) - Rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.
* '''Haiden (Worship Hall)'''
* '''Ubusuna Shrine'''
* '''Kenjo Hall'''
* '''Harai Place'''
* '''Bell Tower''' - A remnant of the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period.
* '''Shrine Office'''

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Property ===
* '''Honden (Main Hall)'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.

== Address ==
〒524-0041 [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube 1-chome 8-8, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]

== Access ==
* 10-minute walk from [[:en:Moriyama Station|Moriyama Station (Shiga)]] on the [[:en:Biwako Line|Biwako Line]], operated by [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]].

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube district, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]].

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika (era)]] 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the [[:en:Ninju|Ninju era]] 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In [[:en:Gangyō|Gangyō]] 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine received devout support from the [[:en:Rokkaku clan|Rokkaku clan]], the governors of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the [[:en:Genki (era)|Tenshō]] era (1570–1573), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] deposited 60 oaths from [[:en:Yasu District, Shiga|Yasu District, Ōmi]] and [[:en:Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District, Ōmi]] at the shrine. In [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] 3 (1594), [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]] donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the area thrived as the site of the [[:en:Moriyama-juku|Moriyama-juku]] post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju no Mori]] (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ([[:en:Amenohoakari|Amenohoakari]], {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Futsunushi|Futsunushi]])
* '''Additional Deities''': [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) - Rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.
* '''Haiden (Worship Hall)'''
* '''Ubusuna Shrine'''
* '''Kenjo Hall'''
* '''Harai Place'''
* '''Bell Tower''' - A remnant of the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period.
* '''Shrine Office'''

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Property ===
* '''Honden (Main Hall)'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.

== Address ==
〒524-0041 [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube 1-chome 8-8, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]

== Access ==
* 10-minute walk from [[:en:Moriyama Station|Moriyama Station (Shiga)]] on the [[:en:Biwako Line|Biwako Line]], operated by [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]].

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju no Mori]] (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ([[:en:Amenohoakari|Amenohoakari]], {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Futsunushi|Futsunushi]])
* '''Additional Deities''': [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) - Rebuilt in [[:en:Meiō|Meiō]] 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.
* '''Haiden (Worship Hall)'''
* '''Ubusuna Shrine'''
* '''Kenjo Hall'''
* '''Harai Place'''
* '''Bell Tower''' - A remnant of the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period.
* '''Shrine Office'''

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Property ===
* '''Honden (Main Hall)'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.

== Address ==
〒524-0041 [[:en:Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama, Shiga]], Katsube 1-chome 8-8, [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]

== Access ==
* 10-minute walk from [[:en:Moriyama Station|Moriyama Station (Shiga)]] on the [[:en:Biwako Line|Biwako Line]], operated by [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]].

{{Infobox religious building

|Shrine
 | name = Katsube Shrine (勝部神社)
 | location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}, {{ill|Moriyama, Shiga|en|Moriyama, Shiga|WD=Q857225|lt=Moriyama, Shiga}}, Katsube 1-chome 8-8
 | position = {{coord|35|3|3|N|135|59|26.5|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Katsube Shrine}}
 | enshrined_deity = Mononobe Futsukami ({{ill|Amenohoakari|en|Amenohoakari|WD=Q10940411|lt=Amenokaguyama no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|en|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi}})
 | established = {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika (era)}} 5 (649)
 | shrine_Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}
 | festivals = Matsuirushi Festival (2nd Saturday of January)
 | main_hall =

| location = {{ill|Moriyama|WD=Q857225}}

 }}

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Moriyama, Shiga|en|Moriyama, Shiga|WD=Q857225|lt=Moriyama, Shiga}}, Katsube district, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika (era)}} 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the {{ill|Ninju|en|Ninju|WD=Q1373077|lt=Ninju era}} 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In {{ill|Gangyō|en|Gangyō|WD=Q747451|lt=Gangyō}} 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the shrine received devout support from the {{ill|Rokkaku clan|en|Rokkaku clan|WD=Q2975342|lt=Rokkaku clan}}, the governors of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in {{ill|Meiō|en|Meiō|WD=Q1193841|lt=Meiō}} 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the {{ill|Genki (era)|en|Genki (era)|WD=Q1145064|lt=Tenshō}} era (1570–1573), {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} deposited 60 oaths from {{ill|Yasu District, Shiga|en|Yasu District, Shiga|WD=Q8049933|lt=Yasu District, Ōmi}} and {{ill|Kurita District, Shiga|en|Kurita District, Shiga|WD=Q6446084|lt=Kurita District, Ōmi}} at the shrine. In {{ill|Bunroku|en|Bunroku|WD=Q1373039|lt=Bunroku}} 3 (1594), {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|en|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|WD=Q967501|lt=Toyotomi Hidetsugu}} donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the area thrived as the site of the {{ill|Moriyama-juku|en|Moriyama-juku|WD=Q3324144|lt=Moriyama-juku}} post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved {{ill|Chinju no Mori|en|Chinju no Mori|WD=Q11651158|lt=Chinju no Mori}} (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ({{ill|Amenohoakari|en|Amenohoakari|WD=Q10940411|lt=Amenohoakari}}, {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|en|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi}})
* '''Additional Deities''': {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}) - Rebuilt in {{ill|Meiō|en|Meiō|WD=Q1193841|lt=Meiō}} 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.
* '''Haiden (Worship Hall)'''
* '''Ubusuna Shrine'''
* '''Kenjo Hall'''
* '''Harai Place'''
* '''Bell Tower''' - A remnant of the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} period.
* '''Shrine Office'''

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Property ===
* '''Honden (Main Hall)'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.

== Address ==
〒524-0041 {{ill|Moriyama, Shiga|en|Moriyama, Shiga|WD=Q857225|lt=Moriyama, Shiga}}, Katsube 1-chome 8-8, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}

== Access ==
* 10-minute walk from {{ill|Moriyama Station|en|Moriyama Station|WD=Q854718|lt=Moriyama Station (Shiga)}} on the {{ill|Biwako Line|en|Biwako Line|WD=Q804135|lt=Biwako Line}}, operated by {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West}}.

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Moriyama, Shiga|en|Moriyama, Shiga|WD=Q857225|lt=Moriyama, Shiga}}, Katsube district, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika (era)}} 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the {{ill|Ninju|en|Ninju|WD=Q1373077|lt=Ninju era}} 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In {{ill|Gangyō|en|Gangyō|WD=Q747451|lt=Gangyō}} 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the shrine received devout support from the {{ill|Rokkaku clan|en|Rokkaku clan|WD=Q2975342|lt=Rokkaku clan}}, the governors of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in {{ill|Meiō|en|Meiō|WD=Q1193841|lt=Meiō}} 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the {{ill|Genki (era)|en|Genki (era)|WD=Q1145064|lt=Tenshō}} era (1570–1573), {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} deposited 60 oaths from {{ill|Yasu District, Shiga|en|Yasu District, Shiga|WD=Q8049933|lt=Yasu District, Ōmi}} and {{ill|Kurita District, Shiga|en|Kurita District, Shiga|WD=Q6446084|lt=Kurita District, Ōmi}} at the shrine. In {{ill|Bunroku|en|Bunroku|WD=Q1373039|lt=Bunroku}} 3 (1594), {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|en|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|WD=Q967501|lt=Toyotomi Hidetsugu}} donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the area thrived as the site of the {{ill|Moriyama-juku|en|Moriyama-juku|WD=Q3324144|lt=Moriyama-juku}} post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved {{ill|Chinju no Mori|en|Chinju no Mori|WD=Q11651158|lt=Chinju no Mori}} (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ({{ill|Amenohoakari|en|Amenohoakari|WD=Q10940411|lt=Amenohoakari}}, {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|en|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi}})
* '''Additional Deities''': {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}) - Rebuilt in {{ill|Meiō|en|Meiō|WD=Q1193841|lt=Meiō}} 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.
* '''Haiden (Worship Hall)'''
* '''Ubusuna Shrine'''
* '''Kenjo Hall'''
* '''Harai Place'''
* '''Bell Tower''' - A remnant of the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} period.
* '''Shrine Office'''

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Property ===
* '''Honden (Main Hall)'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.

== Address ==
〒524-0041 {{ill|Moriyama, Shiga|en|Moriyama, Shiga|WD=Q857225|lt=Moriyama, Shiga}}, Katsube 1-chome 8-8, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}

== Access ==
* 10-minute walk from {{ill|Moriyama Station|en|Moriyama Station|WD=Q854718|lt=Moriyama Station (Shiga)}} on the {{ill|Biwako Line|en|Biwako Line|WD=Q804135|lt=Biwako Line}}, operated by {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West}}.

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Moriyama, Shiga|en|Moriyama, Shiga|WD=Q857225|lt=Moriyama, Shiga}}, Katsube district, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika (era)}} 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the {{ill|Ninju|en|Ninju|WD=Q1373077|lt=Ninju era}} 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In {{ill|Gangyō|en|Gangyō|WD=Q747451|lt=Gangyō}} 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the shrine received devout support from the {{ill|Rokkaku clan|en|Rokkaku clan|WD=Q2975342|lt=Rokkaku clan}}, the governors of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in {{ill|Meiō|en|Meiō|WD=Q1193841|lt=Meiō}} 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the {{ill|Genki (era)|en|Genki (era)|WD=Q1145064|lt=Tenshō}} era (1570–1573), {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} deposited 60 oaths from {{ill|Yasu District, Shiga|en|Yasu District, Shiga|WD=Q8049933|lt=Yasu District, Ōmi}} and {{ill|Kurita District, Shiga|en|Kurita District, Shiga|WD=Q6446084|lt=Kurita District, Ōmi}} at the shrine. In {{ill|Bunroku|en|Bunroku|WD=Q1373039|lt=Bunroku}} 3 (1594), {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|en|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|WD=Q967501|lt=Toyotomi Hidetsugu}} donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the area thrived as the site of the {{ill|Moriyama-juku|en|Moriyama-juku|WD=Q3324144|lt=Moriyama-juku}} post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved {{ill|Chinju no Mori|en|Chinju no Mori|WD=Q11651158|lt=Chinju no Mori}} (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

{{Citation needed|date=December 27, 2013}}

{{Infobox religious building
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| deity                 = deities of its local area
| type                  = [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]]
|Shrine
 | name = Katsube Shrine (勝部神社)
 | location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}, {{ill|Moriyama, Shiga|en|Moriyama, Shiga|WD=Q857225|lt=Moriyama, Shiga}}, Katsube 1-chome 8-8
 | position = {{coord|35|3|3|N|135|59|26.5|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Katsube Shrine}}
 | enshrined_deity = Mononobe Futsukami ({{ill|Amenohoakari|en|Amenohoakari|WD=Q10940411|lt=Amenokaguyama no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|en|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi}})
 | established = {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika (era)}} 5 (649)
 | shrine_Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}
 | festivals = Matsuirushi Festival (2nd Saturday of January)
 | main_hall =
 | image = File:Katsube-jinja02s3200.jpg
| location = {{ill|Moriyama|WD=Q857225}}
 | coordinates = {{Coord|35.05083333333334|N|135.99069444444444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
 }}

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Moriyama, Shiga|en|Moriyama, Shiga|WD=Q857225|lt=Moriyama, Shiga}}, Katsube district, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika (era)}} 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the {{ill|Ninju|en|Ninju|WD=Q1373077|lt=Ninju era}} 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In {{ill|Gangyō|en|Gangyō|WD=Q747451|lt=Gangyō}} 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the shrine received devout support from the {{ill|Rokkaku clan|en|Rokkaku clan|WD=Q2975342|lt=Rokkaku clan}}, the governors of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in {{ill|Meiō|en|Meiō|WD=Q1193841|lt=Meiō}} 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the {{ill|Genki (era)|en|Genki (era)|WD=Q1145064|lt=Tenshō}} era (1570–1573), {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} deposited 60 oaths from {{ill|Yasu District, Shiga|en|Yasu District, Shiga|WD=Q8049933|lt=Yasu District, Ōmi}} and {{ill|Kurita District, Shiga|en|Kurita District, Shiga|WD=Q6446084|lt=Kurita District, Ōmi}} at the shrine. In {{ill|Bunroku|en|Bunroku|WD=Q1373039|lt=Bunroku}} 3 (1594), {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|en|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|WD=Q967501|lt=Toyotomi Hidetsugu}} donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the area thrived as the site of the {{ill|Moriyama-juku|en|Moriyama-juku|WD=Q3324144|lt=Moriyama-juku}} post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved {{ill|Chinju no Mori|en|Chinju no Mori|WD=Q11651158|lt=Chinju no Mori}} (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ({{ill|Amenohoakari|en|Amenohoakari|WD=Q10940411|lt=Amenohoakari}}, {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|en|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi}})
* '''Additional Deities''': {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}) - Rebuilt in {{ill|Meiō|en|Meiō|WD=Q1193841|lt=Meiō}} 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.
* '''Haiden (Worship Hall)'''
* '''Ubusuna Shrine'''
* '''Kenjo Hall'''
* '''Harai Place'''
* '''Bell Tower''' - A remnant of the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} period.
* '''Shrine Office'''

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Important Cultural Property ===
* '''Honden (Main Hall)'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.

== Address ==
〒524-0041 {{ill|Moriyama, Shiga|en|Moriyama, Shiga|WD=Q857225|lt=Moriyama, Shiga}}, Katsube 1-chome 8-8, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}

== Access ==
* 10-minute walk from {{ill|Moriyama Station|en|Moriyama Station|WD=Q854718|lt=Moriyama Station (Shiga)}} on the {{ill|Biwako Line|en|Biwako Line|WD=Q804135|lt=Biwako Line}}, operated by {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West}}.

[[File:Katsube-jinja04s3872.jpg|thumb|200px|Kenjo Hall]]
'''Katsube Shrine''' (勝部神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Moriyama, Shiga|en|Moriyama, Shiga|WD=Q857225|lt=Moriyama, Shiga}}, Katsube district, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}. The shrine's official name is "Mononobe-gō Sōja Katsube Shrine" (物部郷総社 勝部神社). Before the war, it was called "Mononobe Shrine," "Mononobe Daimyōjin," or "Katsube Daimyōjin." It was the [[Sōja shrine]] (総社) of the former Mononobe district and held the Rank of a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

== History ==

In {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika (era)}} 5 (649), on August 13, Mononobe no Sukune Hirokuni enshrined their ancestral deity at the current location in Katsube village of the Mononobe district, naming it "Mononobe Daimyōjin." In the {{ill|Ninju|en|Ninju|WD=Q1373077|lt=Ninju era}} 1 (851), the shrine was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|ja|正六位上|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} in January. In {{ill|Gangyō|en|Gangyō|WD=Q747451|lt=Gangyō}} 6 (882), it received {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}.

During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the shrine received devout support from the {{ill|Rokkaku clan|en|Rokkaku clan|WD=Q2975342|lt=Rokkaku clan}}, the governors of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. When the Rokkaku army set out on campaigns, they always used bamboo from the shrine's bamboo grove for their flags. The three-bay nagare-zukuri style honden with cypress bark roofing was rebuilt in {{ill|Meiō|en|Meiō|WD=Q1193841|lt=Meiō}} 6 (1497) through the contributions of {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}.

In the {{ill|Genki (era)|en|Genki (era)|WD=Q1145064|lt=Tenshō}} era (1570–1573), {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} deposited 60 oaths from {{ill|Yasu District, Shiga|en|Yasu District, Shiga|WD=Q8049933|lt=Yasu District, Ōmi}} and {{ill|Kurita District, Shiga|en|Kurita District, Shiga|WD=Q6446084|lt=Kurita District, Ōmi}} at the shrine. In {{ill|Bunroku|en|Bunroku|WD=Q1373039|lt=Bunroku}} 3 (1594), {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|en|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|WD=Q967501|lt=Toyotomi Hidetsugu}} donated rice fields and repaired the honden.

During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the area thrived as the site of the {{ill|Moriyama-juku|en|Moriyama-juku|WD=Q3324144|lt=Moriyama-juku}} post town.

In 1913, the honden was designated as a National Specially Protected Building, equivalent to the current designation of {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.

Until the Edo period, the shrine was called "Mononobe Shrine" or "Mononobe Daimyōjin," but it was renamed Katsube Shrine in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}. In 1931, it was designated as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The shrine includes a preserved {{ill|Chinju no Mori|en|Chinju no Mori|WD=Q11651158|lt=Chinju no Mori}} (sacred forest) within its grounds.

The Kenjo Hall and Chūkonhi monument were constructed before World War II to honor local war dead.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Primary deity''': Mononobe Futsukami ({{ill|Amenohoakari|en|Amenohoakari|WD=Q10940411|lt=Amenohoakari}}, {{ill|Umashimaji|ja|宇麻志間知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|en|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi}})
* '''Additional Deities''': {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} and [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]

== Grounds ==
* '''Honden (Main Hall)''' ({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}) - Rebuilt in {{ill|Meiō|en|Meiō|WD=Q1193841|lt=Meiō}} 6 (1497) by {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2890348}}. Three-bay nagare-zukuri style with cypress bark roofing.

* '''Shrine Office'''

=== Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property ===
* '''Matsuirushi Festival''' - Commonly known as "Katsube Fire Festival." One of the three major fire festivals in Shiga Prefecture, it boasts a history of 800 years. Held annually on the second Saturday of January, it is preserved by the Katsube Shrine Matsuirushi Group.
